sentences.text: First, I have to say that there was-- before-- right before Purim, there was a committee-- a committee organized in the kehilla that would collect money, which was called for [YIDDISH] which means collect money for flour to bake the matzo for people that are-- that are poor and couldn't-- couldn't afford it.
They would-- they would select a person that would-- I don't know exactly-- they-- I believe that they would have to travel to the city of Khust to get that flour.
And that flour had to be specially-- specially guarded, that-- that there wouldn't be any moisture in it.
And-- and this was always done by, I remember, a-- a-- a poor Jew.
And-- and for that-- for that work, he was-- he was getting-- getting his share of flour.
And they would bring that and store it into an area where they had in the-- in the-- in the synagogue.
And just before-- right after Purim, they would start-- start get-together groups-- four or five families-- and they would-- they would bake it, bake the-- the matzos.
Shabbat-- Shabbat was-- was a-- a-- a totally-- totally-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] how should I say that-- it was a very, very restful time, because it was observed very strictly.
And it is-- it is in-- in Jewish folklore, known as the [YIDDISH] Shabbat, that you got the-- you are getting a additional soul on Shabbos.
Shabbos was very restful and-- and-- and-- and spiritual.
The-- the money-- there wasn't one that-- person, that-- unless he was very ill, that wouldn't-- wouldn't participate in the services.
As I-- I said, this was a-- a Hasidic type of-- of community.
And-- and-- the Hasidic community has a-- a-- special-- special trait, that-- that they are celebrating everything in a-- in a happy mood.
Everything was in a joyful, in a joyful mood.
So those were-- those were the-- the-- the Shabbats, Friday night.
And-- and especially very-- very often, rabbis used to come.
Famous rabbis used to come visit those small communities.
And I'm not talking about rabbis the way we understand them here-- rabbis that are-- are-- are paid-- paid by communities.
I'm talking about rabbis that had followers in the thousands.
I mean, famous rabbis.
They were not a rabbi-- rabbi from Allentown or-- they were [INAUDIBLE]-- they-- they were-- by-- first of all, most-- most of them had-- had to have published some-- some writings about Torah.
And-- and some of them were called by their names-- the names of the-- of the commentaries that they had written.
So-- and another thing-- the Baal Shem movement-- the Hasidic movement-- they believed that some of the people, some of these rabbis are capable of-- of performing miracles.
And people believed in that.
To get a bracha-- you know what a bracha is?
A blessing from a rabbi, it was worth a lot-- a lot of money.
And-- and people believed in that.
And-- and so a lot of them used to come.
I-- I speak about this here, because since we had a big-- a big house, and we had enough room, they used to stay-- most-- most of the time, they used to stay in our house.
And they would stay for a-- for almost a week.
And-- and people from the surrounding the communities that heard that this and this rabbi is visiting here-- they would come-- come to greet the rabbi and to get a blessing.
That was the--
On the-- on the-- on the holidays, yes.
Yes.
I don't-- not always.
No.
No, I don't think she did.
My father was in the lumber business.
We-- we-- we owned a lumber mill.
My father owned a-- a-- over 500 acres of forest and a lot of land.
And-- and-- the-- the business-- the business was selling wholesale and also retail building materials.
The-- the lumber-- the wholesale lumber-- was shipped by river.
Hungary was-- has no lumber whatsoever.
And so they were very hungry for-- for lumber, for building materials.
And the way we-- my father used to the business with company that was in Budapest, in Hungary.
And we were in Czechoslovakia.
But-- when we shipped the lumber to them, by the river Riga, it went 80 kilometers to the city of Khust and, of course, more-- more streams were-- got into this, until it fell into the Tisza River.
The Tisza River was already a big river.
You could ship logs.
They would-- they were tied together, 10 or eight or 10 logs.
And the logs would be like 18, 20 meters-- which is like, meters is-- 20 meters would be about 60 feet long.
It-- this was very, very interesting work.
Whole logs, the way they tie them-- they tie them together with special-- not-- not ropes, but they made-- they made the ropes out of-- out of-- young-- young-- twine-- not twine--
From twigs.
But they burned it.
It was a whole process how they did that.
And-- and once they got into the Tisza, the Tisza River fell into the Danube.
And the Danube already across, went all the way to Budapest.
And that's how-- that was the-- this was a business that-- that-- it was seasonal.
Winter time, you had to cut the trees in the summer.
But you couldn't take it out in the summer from the forests.
Winter, when the snow-- the snow was-- on the ground, and the Carpathians especially, we had a long-- long winter.
So we would-- we would hire people with horses and even people that have oxen and sleighs.
And to bring that lumber out-- and they would put it alongside-- we had ports, like my father had, that would lay it out alongside the River Riga.
And then in the summer time, we hired laborers to-- to tie these together.
And of course, they were launched, put into the water.
And of course, in the summer time, sometimes when there was no rain, you couldn't-- the-- the waters were not-- not big enough.
And one year, I think in the '30s, they built a dam, and-- and collected water so that they can release water whenever they need it in order to-- to be able to ship it [INAUDIBLE].
But this was the-- the-- the main industry.
Well, I was-- I was too young-- too, too young.
The only-- the only thing that I-- I knew about was I used to love to be on the-- on the-- on the saw mill work.
That's where they-- where they cut logs and-- and cut them in boards.
And then they split-- split them, make two by twos or two by fours, or whatever they-- they worked it up.
That-- and that was-- that was a water-- water-driven mill.
But it had a turbine.
And-- and that's how-- that's how-- and it worked 24 hours, day and night.
And even winter time, where there-- most of the river would-- would freeze sometimes, but there was enough water where we had the mill, that there was enough water to drive-- to drive it.
Very-- I don't remember it ever being stopped in winter.
The-- my father lost a lot of money.
In 1929, I was nine years old.
I remember we paid-- we paid 100 or 120 krones for a cubic meter of lumber.
And two years later, in 1930, '31, when the-- when the depression came, you couldn't sell it for 40 krone, for a third.
And-- and what the trouble was, that you had thousands of cubic meters of lumber laying out by the river.
And a-- a-- there was like a-- a-- it's not a microbe-- some kind of a-- a-- thing would get into the-- into the trees.
And it--
It wasn't a bacteria.
It was-- it was green.
It was like-- like a-- how-- how-- like a little worm, green worms.
And, you know, and-- and-- and it-- it would already-- it would already-- and if it laid for-- for two years, summer and winter out there, it deteriorated.
And the quality deteriorated.
And that was-- that was the-- the problem.
The first effects of the war we started to feel in 1935-- '34, '35.
The-- the first two families that-- that escaped from Germany in 1945-- '35-- from-- the two families were from Frankfurt am Main.
And I remember they came to Czechoslovakia.
How they came to Torun, I don't know.
But I remember one family stayed with us.

And that was the first time-- you know, I was about 15-years-old-- I-- I really started to realize what was going on, that the-- they were talking in the house, that his family was living in-- in-- in Frankfurt, in the city.
And they had to leave or how exactly, I don't-- I don't remember.
But that they were refugees, they lost.
That's how it first started to affect me-- as-- as to realize that something is wrong.
And-- and-- we-- we didn't-- in Czechoslovakia, in-- at that time, we didn't-- we didn't feel anti-Semitism.
And for the simple reason because the Czech government knew that there is 10% of the population that was in Carpathia was Jewish.
You have to remember that the Carpathian area was included in Czechoslovakia under the agreement of the-- of the first [?
Felkerbund ?] that was organized.
Like the United Nations was organized after World War II, the [?
Felkerbund ?] was organized after World War I.
And that's when the-- the-- the Austrian-Hungarian empire was-- fell apart and-- and the Czechoslovakia was-- was-- came to existence as an independent state.
But the Ruthenia, that Carpathian area, was given to them under a autonomy.
They had full-- they had full autonomy.
So they-- they had to have-- they knew that maybe they have different aspirations.
So the-- they knew that the Jewish people had no-- no aspirations for-- for themselves in that autonomy, except to live under a democratic country.
So-- and they-- so we-- we enjoyed, more or less, a-- a-- they knew that we were loyal citizens.
And they needed us.
Well, the Zionist movement was to, first of all, to-- to learn Hebrew, number one.
And number two, it was the movement to-- to return to Zion.

It-- it was-- and while we are at the subject of return to Zion, I have to tell you that my great-grandfather, after whom I am named, that means my mother's grandfather-- his name was Michael Drimmer.
He was the first Jew that emigrated to Palestine in 1903.
He was-- he was in his early 60s.
He was such a [NON-ENGLISH].
He-- he-- and this is more or less-- he-- he felt a messianic mission that he was able to-- he left his entire family, his children, whatever he owned.
He left with his wife and went to Jerusalem.
And at that time, the Ottoman Empire-- the-- the-- the Turkey-- had-- Palestine was under the Ottoman Empire control.
So the-- the-- the belief in the return-- that the Jewish people will return to-- to Zion was so strong in him.
And he was such a-- such a-- in our-- in our area, they talked a lot about him, because he was-- he came-- he was born in Dolyna, and on the other side-- well, that was Austria-Hungary.
It was one country before World War I.
And he used to be the-- the-- the cantor for the high holidays.
Of course, he-- he did that as-- as-- that wasn't a paid-- paid-- paid position.
It was an honor to-- to be-- to be the-- the cantor.
And he brought over the melodies that were-- that were used in-- in Galicia and-- and that area, when he came over to live in the-- in the Carpathians.
And in 1903, he got up, and he left.
My uncle, [INAUDIBLE], where I was with him in 1941, he told me the story that he helped him.
He helped get the papers.
And at that time, they were going from Romania to the Black Sea, into the-- the Dardanelles and the Mediterranean.
That must have taken him-- I don't know how long it took at that time to get-- to get to Palestine.
He came and settled in Jerusalem.
And he was-- he lived there.
He died in 1917.
He lived 14 years after-- after he emigrated.
And when I-- I was in Israel visiting, five, six years ago, I-- I managed to-- to find somebody that was taking care-- has the books of the cemetery of the [NON-ENGLISH], of the Mount of Olives.
And I asked him to look up the name.
And he found the name and the date and everything-- when he-- when he died.
My older brothers, Eliezer and-- and Moshe, were-- were-- they always used to brag that they were very proud that they got their grandfather-- their great-grandfather sent them Tefillin.
When they got bar mitzvah'd-- well, they were bar mitzvah'd already-- he was already in Israel-- in Israel-- in Palestine, Jerusalem a few years.
They-- he sent them Tefillin.
And they were very proud of that, that they got Tefillin.
We also used to talk-- I remember now, our house-- that for some time they would for the holiday of Sukkot, that he would send a-- a-- a esrog.
You know what an esrog is?
An esrog and lulav, that-- this was very unique at that time, that you had an esrog coming from Israel.
It's not like now, that you put it on the plane, and they bring it over here.
It's here.
And in those years-- What was your involvement in the Zionist movement?
Well, I was a Zionist.
I believed in it.
And I also wanted-- wanted to-- to leave.
When I was about 16, I wanted to-- to go to the-- there was a-- a Hashara.
You know what a Hashara means?
Preparation places, where they-- they would teach people traits.
The-- the revisionist organization to which I belonged had organized a marine school, to teach Jewish people how to work on-- on ships.
And I was-- I was 16-years-old at that time, and I wanted to leave.
But I was the baby of the family.
My older two brothers were married, and they had their own children.
They lived out-- out-- out in their own houses.
And my parents just wouldn't-- wouldn't let me go.
And this was one of my disappointments.
And throughout the war-- throughout the war time, my-- and my older brother, also that wasn't married at that time yet, Tsvi, he also wanted to go.
And-- and-- and again, my parents were very much against it.
And during the war time, and I-- all those bitter and terrible nights and days that I had to endure, I-- I always accused myself.
I was mad at myself.
This-- all this here is-- I deserve it because I shouldn't-- I shouldn't have listened.
I should have left.
OK.
